missions.title: Build Your First Website
missions.description: Build and deploy a personal portfolio website using Claude as your AI coding partner. No coding experience needed.
missions.project_brief: You'll build a real portfolio website from scratch using nothing but conversations with Claude. By the end, you'll have a live site on the internet with your name on it — and you'll understand HTML, CSS, and JavaScript well enough to keep improving it.
learning_outcomes: Write effective prompts that get Claude to generate exactly what you want
Understand HTML structure, CSS styling, and JavaScript interactivity
Use Claude Code to refactor and polish a real project
Deploy a live website to Vercel

steps.title: Meet Your AI Partner
steps.description: Before we build anything, let's meet Claude and understand what it can do.

steps.title: Your First Prompt
steps.description: Learn to talk to Claude the right way — clear, specific, and effective.

steps.title: Design Your Homepage
steps.description: Ask Claude to generate HTML for your portfolio. Your first real code.

sections.content:
Time to Build

You've learned how to talk to Claude. Now let's build something real. By the end of this step, you'll have a working webpage that you wrote — with Claude's help.

What is HTML? HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the language that every webpage is written in. It's not a programming language — it's a markup language, meaning it describes the structure of content. Think of it like an outline: headings, paragraphs, images, links. Your browser reads the HTML and renders it visually.

Step 1: Describe Yourself

Open Claude and write a prompt that describes who you are and what your portfolio should showcase. Be specific. Here's a template to start from:

I'm building a personal portfolio website. Here's what I need:

- My name: [Your Name]
- What I do: [Your role or passion]
- 3 things I want to highlight: [list them]
- Tone: [professional / creative / minimal / bold]

Generate a complete, single-file HTML page with inline CSS. Include a hero section with my name and tagline, an "About" section, and a footer. Use a modern, clean design with a color palette that fits my tone.

The more detail you give Claude, the better the result. Don't just say "make me a website" — tell it exactly what you want.

Step 2: Save and Preview Your HTML

Copy Claude's response into a new file called index.html on your computer. Open it in your web browser (just double-click the file). You should see your portfolio.

What to look for:

  • Does the page have a clear structure? (Header, sections, footer)
  • Can you find the <h1> tag with your name? That's the main heading.
  • See the <style> block at the top? That's CSS — it controls colors, fonts, spacing.
  • Every piece of text is wrapped in tags like <p> (paragraph), <h2> (subheading), <a> (link)

Step 3: Iterate on the Design

Your first version won't be perfect — and that's the point. Go back to Claude and ask for changes:

The portfolio looks good but I want to change a few things:
1. Make the hero section taller with a gradient background
2. Add more spacing between sections
3. Change the font to something more modern

Here's my current HTML:
[paste your code]

This is the core skill of working with Claude: iterate. Generate, review, request changes, repeat. Each round teaches you more about how the code works because you're reading it, spotting what to change, and describing the fix in plain English.

What You Just Learned

Without writing a single line of code by hand, you now have a working HTML page. But more importantly, you learned:

  • HTML structure: Tags like <div>, <h1>, <p> define what's on the page
  • CSS basics: The <style> block controls how things look
  • Prompt iteration: Getting great output from Claude is a conversation, not a single message

Save your index.html file. We'll keep building on it.

steps.title: System Prompts: Your Web Design Assistant
steps.description: Make Claude behave like a dedicated web designer who knows your style.

steps.title: Add Your Projects Section
steps.description: Use system prompts to make Claude a web designer, then add a projects grid to your portfolio.

sections.content:
Level Up Your Portfolio

Your homepage exists, but a portfolio needs projects. In this step, you'll use what you learned about system prompts to make Claude a better web design partner — and you'll learn CSS Grid, the layout system that powers modern websites.

Step 1: Set Up Your Web Designer

Before asking Claude to write more code, set up a system prompt (or start a new Project in Claude) that primes it as your designer:

You are a senior web designer helping me build my personal portfolio. You write clean, semantic HTML5 with modern CSS. You prefer CSS Grid and Flexbox for layouts. You use a consistent color palette and spacing system. When I ask for changes, you return the complete updated HTML file so I can copy-paste it directly. You explain any new CSS concepts briefly so I learn as we go.

Now Claude will consistently produce clean, well-structured code and teach you along the way. This is the power of system prompts — you set the behavior once and it persists.

Step 2: Build the Projects Grid

Ask Claude to add a projects section to your existing HTML:

Add a "Projects" section to my portfolio with a responsive grid layout. I want to showcase 3-4 projects. Each project card should have:
- A colored placeholder where an image would go
- Project title
- Short description (1-2 sentences)
- A "View Project" link

Use CSS Grid so the cards arrange in 3 columns on desktop and stack on mobile. Here are my projects:
1. [Your project or interest]
2. [Another one]
3. [A third one]

Here's my current HTML:
[paste your index.html]

Step 3: Understand What Changed

Look at the new code Claude generated. Find these CSS concepts:

  • display: grid — This turns a container into a grid layout
  • grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr) — Creates 3 equal columns. 1fr means "1 fraction of available space"
  • gap: 2rem — Spacing between grid items. rem is a unit relative to the root font size
  • @media (max-width: 768px) — A media query that changes the layout on smaller screens

Try changing repeat(3, 1fr) to repeat(2, 1fr) in your file and refresh the browser. The grid switches to 2 columns. Change it back when you're done — but now you understand how it works.

Step 4: Polish the Cards

Ask Claude to enhance the project cards:

Make the project cards more polished:
- Add a subtle box shadow
- Add a hover effect (slight lift and shadow change)
- Round the corners
- Make the "View Project" link look like a button

After you paste in the updated code, hover over a card in your browser. See the animation? That's CSS transition and :hover pseudo-class at work. You just learned interactive CSS without writing a line of it yourself.

steps.title: Make It Interactive
steps.description: Add a contact form and dark mode toggle. Your first JavaScript.

sections.content:
Adding Interactivity

HTML defines structure. CSS defines appearance. JavaScript defines behavior — what happens when someone clicks, scrolls, or types. In this step, you'll add two interactive features: a contact form and a dark mode toggle.

Step 1: Add a Contact Form

Ask Claude:

Add a contact form section to my portfolio before the footer. Include fields for:
- Name (required)
- Email (required)
- Message (textarea, required)
- A submit button

Style it to match the rest of the site. Add basic form validation — show a red border on empty required fields when the user tries to submit. For now, just show an alert that says "Message sent!" when the form is valid (we'll connect it to a real backend later).

Here's my current HTML:
[paste your code]

Look for the <form> tag and the <script> block in the response. The script is JavaScript. Find addEventListener — that's how JavaScript listens for user actions like button clicks.

Step 2: Add Dark Mode

This is the fun one. Ask Claude:

Add a dark mode toggle button to my portfolio. Requirements:
- Put a sun/moon icon button in the top-right corner of the header
- Clicking it switches between light and dark themes
- Save the user's preference in localStorage so it persists
- Use CSS custom properties (variables) for all colors so the switch is clean
- Animate the transition smoothly

Here's my current HTML:
[paste your code]

Step 3: Understand the JavaScript

Find these patterns in the dark mode code Claude generated:

  • :root { --bg-color: #ffffff; } — CSS custom properties (variables). Instead of hardcoding colors everywhere, you define them once and reference them with var(--bg-color)
  • document.body.classList.toggle('dark') — This adds or removes the CSS class dark from the body, which triggers different variable values
  • localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark') — This saves data in the browser that persists even after the page is closed
  • window.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', ...) — This runs code when the page first loads, checking if the user previously chose dark mode

Toggle dark mode on and off. Close the browser, reopen the file. Your preference was saved. That's localStorage at work.

What You Just Learned

You now have a portfolio with real interactivity. The key JavaScript concepts you've seen in action:

  • Event listeners: Code that runs when the user does something
  • DOM manipulation: Changing what's on the page with JavaScript
  • localStorage: Saving data in the browser
  • Form validation: Checking user input before processing it

Your single HTML file is getting powerful. In the next steps, we'll level up to professional tools.

steps.title: Getting Started with Claude Code
steps.description: Level up from copy-pasting to using Claude Code in your terminal.

steps.title: Polish with Claude Code
steps.description: Use Claude Code to refactor your site, add responsive design, and fix bugs.

sections.content:
From Copy-Paste to Real Development

Up until now, you've been copying code from Claude into a file manually. That works, but professionals use better tools. Claude Code runs in your terminal and can read, write, and edit your files directly. It sees your entire project and can make surgical changes without you needing to paste anything.

Step 1: Open Your Project in Claude Code

Open your terminal, navigate to the folder where your index.html lives, and start Claude Code:

cd ~/my-portfolio
claude

Claude Code now sees your project. Try asking:

What files are in this directory? Read index.html and tell me what you see.

Claude Code will read your file and give you a summary. It can see everything in your project — no pasting required.

Step 2: Split Into Multiple Files

Professional websites don't put everything in one file. Ask Claude Code:

Refactor my index.html into a proper project structure:
- index.html (just the HTML, linking to external files)
- styles.css (all the CSS)
- script.js (all the JavaScript)

Keep everything working exactly as it does now.

Claude Code will create the new files and update the HTML to link to them. Refresh your browser — everything should look identical. But now your code is organized like a real project.

Step 3: Add Responsive Design

Your site might look good on your laptop, but what about phones? Ask Claude Code:

Make my portfolio fully responsive. Test these breakpoints:
- Mobile (under 480px): Single column, smaller fonts, hamburger menu idea
- Tablet (480-768px): Two-column grid
- Desktop (768px+): Current layout

Also add proper meta viewport tag if it's missing, and make images/containers use max-width: 100% so nothing overflows.

After Claude Code makes the changes, resize your browser window to see the layout adapt. That's responsive design — the page reorganizes itself based on screen size.

Step 4: Fix Bugs and Polish

Ask Claude Code to do a quality check:

Review my portfolio code for:
- Accessibility issues (missing alt text, poor contrast, no semantic HTML)
- Performance issues (unnecessarily large CSS, render-blocking resources)
- Cross-browser compatibility
- Any bugs

Fix everything you find and explain what you changed.

This is one of Claude Code's superpowers — code review. It will catch things you'd never notice as a beginner: missing alt attributes on images (important for screen readers), poor color contrast ratios, non-semantic HTML tags. Read its explanations. Each fix teaches you a best practice.

steps.title: Go Live on Vercel
steps.description: Deploy your portfolio to the internet. Your site gets a real URL.

sections.content:
Your Site Goes Live

Your portfolio is polished, responsive, and interactive. It's been living on your computer. Now it's time to put it on the internet where anyone can see it.

What is Vercel? Vercel is a hosting platform that makes deployment dead simple. You push your code, and Vercel builds and hosts it automatically. It's free for personal projects and gives you a real URL.

Step 1: Create Your Accounts

You need two things:

  1. GitHub account — Go to github.com and sign up if you haven't. GitHub stores your code in the cloud.
  2. Vercel account — Go to vercel.com and sign up with your GitHub account. This links them together.

Step 2: Push to GitHub

In your terminal (not Claude Code), set up Git and push your project:

cd ~/my-portfolio
git init
git add .
git commit -m "My portfolio website"
gh repo create my-portfolio --public --push

If you don't have the GitHub CLI (gh), ask Claude Code to help you install it, or create the repository manually on github.com and follow the instructions to push an existing repo.

Step 3: Deploy on Vercel

Two options — pick whichever feels more comfortable:

Option A: Vercel Dashboard (visual)

  1. Go to vercel.com/new
  2. Import your GitHub repository
  3. Vercel auto-detects it as a static site
  4. Click "Deploy"
  5. Wait 30 seconds. You'll get a URL like my-portfolio-abc123.vercel.app

Option B: Vercel CLI (terminal)

npm i -g vercel
cd ~/my-portfolio
vercel

Follow the prompts. When it's done, you'll get your URL printed in the terminal.

Step 4: Visit Your Live Site

Open the URL Vercel gave you. That's your website. On the internet. Anyone in the world can see it right now.

Try opening it on your phone. Since we added responsive design, it should look great on every screen size.

What just happened: Vercel pulled your code from GitHub, detected it was a static site (plain HTML/CSS/JS), and deployed it to their global CDN (Content Delivery Network). Your site is now served from data centers around the world, loading fast for everyone.

Every time you push a change to GitHub, Vercel will automatically redeploy. That's continuous deployment — a practice used by every professional engineering team.

steps.title: Your Portfolio is Live!
steps.description: Review what you built, what you learned, and share your URL with the world.

sections.content:
Mission Complete

Take a moment to look at what you've accomplished. You started this mission having never written a line of code. Now you have:

  • A live portfolio website with your name on it
  • A responsive design that works on every device
  • Interactive features: contact form, dark mode
  • Clean, organized code split into HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files
  • A GitHub repository with version control
  • Continuous deployment on Vercel

Skills Unlocked

  • Prompt Engineering: You can describe what you want and get Claude to build it
  • HTML: You understand page structure, tags, semantic markup
  • CSS: You know about Grid, Flexbox, custom properties, media queries, transitions
  • JavaScript: You've seen event listeners, DOM manipulation, localStorage
  • Claude Code: You can use the terminal to work on projects directly
  • Deployment: You can ship code to the internet with Git and Vercel

Share Your Work

Share your portfolio URL. Post it on LinkedIn, X, or wherever you hang out online. You built this. That's worth celebrating.

Next steps to consider:

  • Add a custom domain (Vercel makes this easy — check their Domains settings)
  • Add a blog section where you write about what you're learning
  • Upgrade to a framework like Next.js (spoiler: Mission 4 covers this)
  • Keep iterating with Claude Code — the more you use it, the faster you build

missions.title: Automate Your Workflow
missions.description: Build a personal productivity automation that saves you 5+ hours per week. No coding required — just Claude and smart prompt engineering.
missions.project_brief: You'll identify your most repetitive work tasks, build reusable prompt templates for each one, chain them into a complete workflow, and document it as a standard operating procedure your team can use. By the end, you'll have a system that runs on autopilot.
learning_outcomes: Audit your own workflow for automation opportunities
Build reusable prompt templates with variables
Chain multiple AI tasks into end-to-end workflows
Write SOPs that make your automation reproducible

steps.title: Create Your AI Work Assistant
steps.description: Use system prompts to build a Claude persona tailored to your specific job.

steps.title: Map Your Repetitive Tasks
steps.description: Audit your workweek and identify the 3 biggest time-wasters Claude can handle.

sections.content:
Find Your Automation Opportunities

Before we build anything, we need to find the right things to automate. The biggest productivity mistake people make with AI is automating the wrong tasks — things that are already fast, or things that require too much judgment.

The sweet spot: tasks that are repetitive, structured, and take 15+ minutes each time.

Step 1: The Time Audit

Think through your last work week. Write down every task that felt repetitive. Be specific — don't write "emails," write "drafting follow-up emails to clients after calls." Use this prompt with Claude to help:

I'm a [your role] at a [type of company]. Help me identify my most automatable tasks.

Here's what my typical week looks like:
- [Monday routine]
- [Tuesday routine]
- [etc.]

For each task I mention, rate it on:
1. Repetitiveness (1-5): How similar is it each time?
2. Structure (1-5): How predictable is the format?
3. Time cost (minutes per occurrence)
4. Frequency (times per week)

Then rank the top 5 by total time saved potential.

Step 2: Pick Your Top 3

From Claude's analysis, pick the top 3 tasks. Good candidates include:

  • Writing meeting summaries or action items
  • Drafting emails from bullet points
  • Reformatting data between systems
  • Creating reports from raw numbers
  • Writing documentation or SOPs
  • Reviewing and editing content
  • Generating social media posts from longer content

Write down each task with: what triggers it, what input it needs, what the output looks like, and how long it takes you today.

Step 3: Define Your Baseline

For each of your 3 tasks, calculate your current weekly time cost:

Task 1: [name] — [X] minutes × [Y] times/week = [Z] minutes/week
Task 2: [name] — [X] minutes × [Y] times/week = [Z] minutes/week
Task 3: [name] — [X] minutes × [Y] times/week = [Z] minutes/week
Total: [sum] minutes/week

This is your baseline. By the end of this mission, you'll measure your actual time savings against it. If the total is under 2 hours, go back and find bigger tasks — the best automations save at least 5 hours per week.

steps.title: Build Your First Template
steps.description: Create a reusable prompt template for your biggest time-waster and test it.

sections.content:
From Ad-Hoc Prompts to Reusable Templates

Most people use Claude by typing a new prompt from scratch every time. That's like rewriting a recipe from memory every time you cook. Instead, we're going to build prompt templates — reusable prompts with variables you swap out each time.

Step 1: Write the First Draft

Take your #1 time-waster from the previous step. Open Claude and write the best prompt you can to accomplish it. Don't worry about making it reusable yet — just make it work. Run it with a real example from your work.

Example: If your task is "write meeting follow-up emails," your first draft might be:

Here are my notes from a client meeting:
[paste actual notes]

Write a professional follow-up email that:
- Thanks them for their time
- Summarizes the key discussion points
- Lists agreed-upon action items with owners
- Proposes next meeting date
- Keeps the tone warm but professional

Step 2: Test with 3 Real Examples

Run your prompt with 3 different real examples from your work. For each one, note:

  • What did Claude get right on the first try?
  • What did you have to correct or re-prompt for?
  • What was missing that you had to add manually?

These notes reveal the gaps in your template. If Claude consistently misses the tone, you need better tone instructions. If it forgets to include certain details, you need to require them explicitly.

Step 3: Convert to a Template

Now generalize your prompt. Replace the specific details with variables in brackets:

MEETING FOLLOW-UP EMAIL TEMPLATE

Context:
- Client: {{client_name}} at {{company}}
- Meeting date: {{date}}
- Attendees: {{attendees}}
- My role: {{my_role}}

Meeting notes:
{{raw_notes}}

Write a follow-up email that:
- Thanks them for their time (use first name)
- Summarizes 3-5 key discussion points from the notes
- Lists all action items with owner and deadline
- Proposes a follow-up meeting for {{follow_up_timeframe}}
- Tone: warm, professional, concise
- Length: under 300 words
- Subject line format: "Follow-up: {{meeting_topic}} — Action Items"

Save this template somewhere accessible — a notes app, a text file, or a Claude Project. You'll use it every time this task comes up.

Step 4: Measure the Improvement

Use your template on the next real occurrence of this task. Time yourself from start (pasting the template and filling in variables) to finish (final email ready to send). Compare it to your baseline.

A good template should cut the task time by 60-80%. If it doesn't, the template needs more work — go back and add more specificity, better examples, or stricter formatting requirements.

steps.title: Chaining Tasks Together
steps.description: Learn how to connect multiple Claude tasks into an end-to-end workflow.

steps.title: Chain Your Templates
steps.description: Connect your 3 templates into one workflow. One input triggers a chain of outputs.

sections.content:
Building a Workflow Chain

Individual templates save time. Chained templates save entire processes. A workflow chain means the output of one step becomes the input of the next — you provide the initial input once, and the chain produces multiple outputs.

Step 1: Map the Chain

Look at your 3 templates. Are any of them naturally connected? Common chains:

  • Meeting notes → follow-up email → task list → calendar invites
  • Raw data → analysis → report → executive summary
  • Client brief → project plan → timeline → kickoff email
  • Support ticket → research → draft response → internal note

Draw your chain on paper or in a doc: Input → Template 1 → Output 1 → Template 2 → Output 2 → Template 3 → Final Output

If your 3 templates don't naturally chain, create a new connecting template. For example, if Template 1 produces a meeting summary and Template 2 needs a task list, add a step that extracts tasks from the summary.

Step 2: Build the Chain in Claude

Open a Claude conversation and run your chain end-to-end. Start with a real input:

I'm going to run a 3-step workflow. After each step, I'll say "next" and you'll use the output from the previous step as input for the next one.

STEP 1: Meeting Follow-Up Email
[paste your Template 1 with real data filled in]

When Claude produces the output, review it, then say:

Next.

STEP 2: Extract Action Items
[paste your Template 2, referencing the email above]

Continue through all 3 steps. At the end, you should have multiple useful outputs from a single input.

Step 3: Create the Mega-Template

Now combine everything into one prompt that runs the whole chain at once:

WORKFLOW: Post-Meeting Processing

Input:
- Client: {{client_name}}
- Meeting notes: {{raw_notes}}
- Attendees: {{attendees}}

Produce ALL of the following:

1. FOLLOW-UP EMAIL
[requirements from Template 1]

2. ACTION ITEM TRACKER
[requirements from Template 2]

3. INTERNAL SUMMARY
[requirements from Template 3]

Format each section with a clear header. Output all three in one response.

Test this mega-template with a real example. Compare the quality to running each step individually. Sometimes a single prompt produces slightly lower quality than running each step separately — if so, keep them as separate steps but document the chain order.

When to Chain vs. When to Keep Separate

Use a single mega-prompt when:

  • The steps are simple and Claude handles all of them well
  • You want maximum speed (one prompt = one response)
  • The outputs don't need individual review between steps

Keep steps separate when:

  • Each step is complex and needs the full context window
  • You need to review or edit the output before the next step
  • Different steps need different system prompts or personas

steps.title: Building SOPs with Claude
steps.description: Learn to document your automation so anyone on your team can use it.

steps.title: Write Your SOP
steps.description: Turn your workflow into a standard operating procedure anyone can follow.

sections.content:
Make It Repeatable

An automation that only works when you run it isn't really an automation — it's a personal trick. A Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) turns your workflow into something anyone on your team can execute consistently. It's the difference between "I have a good prompt" and "We have a system."

Step 1: Document the Workflow

Use Claude to help you write the SOP. Here's a proven structure:

Help me write an SOP for my workflow. Here's what it does:

Workflow name: {{name}}
Purpose: {{what problem it solves}}
Trigger: {{when should someone run this}}
Input required: {{what data/info is needed}}
Steps: {{your chain from the previous exercise}}
Output: {{what gets produced}}
Quality check: {{how to verify the output is good}}

Write the SOP with:
- A clear title and purpose statement
- Prerequisites (what you need before starting)
- Numbered steps with exact prompts to copy-paste
- Expected output for each step
- Common issues and how to fix them
- Quality checklist before marking done

Step 2: Include the Actual Prompts

The most important part of your SOP is the exact prompts to use. Don't paraphrase — include them verbatim with the variable placeholders. Someone following the SOP should be able to fill in the blanks and get the same quality output you get.

For each prompt in the SOP, include:

  • The complete prompt text with {{variables}} marked
  • What to fill in for each variable
  • An example of good input for that variable
  • What the output should look like (include a sample)

Step 3: Test It with Someone Else

The real test of an SOP: can someone who's never done this task follow your document and get a good result? Find a colleague, friend, or even use a fresh Claude conversation and pretend you're following the SOP for the first time.

Note where you get confused, where the instructions are ambiguous, and where the output quality drops. Update the SOP based on this feedback. A good SOP usually goes through 2-3 revisions before it's solid.

steps.title: Add Quality Checks
steps.description: Add verification steps that catch errors before they reach anyone.

sections.content:
Trust, But Verify

Claude is powerful but imperfect. Every automation needs quality checks — steps that verify the output meets your standards before it goes anywhere. This is especially important when the output reaches clients, gets published, or feeds into other systems.

Step 1: Identify Failure Modes

For each step in your workflow, ask yourself: "What could go wrong?" Common failure modes with Claude:

  • Hallucinated details: Claude invents facts that weren't in the input
  • Tone drift: The output doesn't match your professional voice
  • Missing items: Claude drops some input data in the output
  • Format errors: Dates, names, or numbers are wrong
  • Over-verbosity: Output is twice as long as it should be

Write down the 2-3 most likely failure modes for your specific workflow.

Step 2: Build Verification Prompts

Create a verification prompt that checks the output against the input:

QUALITY CHECK

Original input:
{{paste the original input}}

Generated output:
{{paste Claude's output}}

Verify:
1. Every fact in the output can be traced to the input (no hallucinations)
2. All names, dates, and numbers match exactly
3. No input items were dropped or omitted
4. Tone is [your target tone]
5. Length is under [your word limit]

For each check, respond PASS or FAIL with a specific explanation. If any check fails, provide the corrected version.

Run this as the final step of your workflow. Yes, you're using Claude to check Claude — but a focused verification prompt catches most issues because it's looking at the output with fresh eyes and specific criteria.

Step 3: Add the Checks to Your SOP

Update your SOP to include the quality check as a mandatory final step. Add a section called "Quality Checklist" that the person running the workflow must complete:

  • [ ] All facts verified against source material
  • [ ] Names and numbers are correct
  • [ ] Tone matches our standard
  • [ ] Output is the right length
  • [ ] Verification prompt returned all PASS

This turns your automation from "generate and hope" to "generate, verify, and ship." It's the difference between amateur and professional AI use.

steps.title: Roll It Out
steps.description: Package your workflow for your team. Create a shared prompt library.

sections.content:
From Personal Tool to Team System

Your workflow is built, tested, and documented. Now it's time to share it. "Deployment" for a workflow automation means making it accessible and usable by your team — not just you.

Step 1: Create a Shared Prompt Library

Organize your templates into a library your team can access. Options by team size:

  • Just you: A Claude Project with all templates pinned as project instructions
  • Small team (2-10): A shared doc (Notion, Google Docs, or a GitHub repo) with all templates organized by use case
  • Larger team: A structured prompt library with categories, versioning, and an owner for each template

At minimum, your library should include:

  • Each template with clear naming (meeting-followup-v2, report-generator-v1)
  • The system prompt / persona for each workflow
  • Example inputs and outputs
  • The SOP document

Step 2: Onboard Your First User

Pick one teammate and walk them through the workflow. Don't just send them the SOP — sit with them (or share a screen) while they run it for the first time. Watch for:

  • Where do they hesitate or ask questions?
  • Do they fill in the variables correctly?
  • Is the output quality as good as when you run it?
  • How long does it take them vs. doing it manually?

This first onboarding session will reveal every gap in your documentation. Update the SOP immediately.

Step 3: Set Up a Feedback Loop

Workflows decay without maintenance. Prompts that work today might need tuning as Claude updates, your processes change, or edge cases emerge. Set up a simple feedback system:

  • A shared channel or doc where team members can flag issues
  • A monthly 15-minute review to check: Is it still saving time? Any new failure modes? Any templates that need updating?
  • Version your templates (v1, v2, v3) so you can roll back if a change makes things worse

steps.title: You Just Saved 5 Hours/Week
steps.description: Calculate your real time savings and plan your next automation.

sections.content:
Mission Complete

Let's measure the impact. Go back to your baseline from Step 2 and compare:

BEFORE:
Task 1: [X] min/week
Task 2: [X] min/week
Task 3: [X] min/week
Total: [X] min/week

AFTER (with automation):
Task 1: [Y] min/week (includes running template + QC)
Task 2: [Y] min/week
Task 3: [Y] min/week
Total: [Y] min/week

TIME SAVED: [X - Y] min/week = [hours] hours/week
ANNUAL SAVINGS: [hours × 50] hours/year

What You Built

  • 3 reusable prompt templates that produce consistent, high-quality output
  • A chained workflow that turns one input into multiple outputs
  • A quality verification system that catches errors before they reach anyone
  • A documented SOP your team can follow without you
  • A shared prompt library that scales your knowledge

Skills Unlocked

  • Workflow analysis: You can identify what to automate (and what not to)
  • Template design: You can build reusable prompts with variables
  • Task chaining: You can connect multiple AI steps into a pipeline
  • Quality assurance: You can verify AI output systematically
  • Knowledge transfer: You can document and share your automations

Next mission: If you're ready to build something more technical — an actual AI agent that uses tools and APIs — Mission 3 is waiting for you.

missions.title: Build an AI Agent
missions.description: Build a working AI agent with tool use, deployed and accessible via API. For developers who want to build real AI products.

Starting from a single API call, you'll progressively add tool use, MCP integration, and Agent SDK architecture until you have a production-ready agent with monitoring and cost tracking.
