steps.title: Your First API Call
steps.description: Make your first Claude API call. The foundation of everything you'll build.

steps.title: Design Your Agent
steps.description: Define what your agent does, what tools it needs, and how it handles errors.

sections.content:
Think Before You Build

The difference between a toy demo and a useful agent is design. Before writing a line of code, you need to answer four questions: What does this agent do? What tools does it need? What can go wrong? How will users interact with it?

Step 1: Pick Your Agent's Purpose

Your agent should solve a real problem you have. Good starter agents:

  • Research agent: Takes a topic, searches the web, summarizes findings
  • Code review agent: Takes a PR URL, reads the diff, provides feedback
  • Data agent: Takes a question, queries a database, returns an answer in plain English
  • Content agent: Takes a brief, generates drafts across multiple formats
  • Support agent: Takes a customer question, searches docs, drafts a response

Pick one. Write a one-sentence description: "My agent [does what] for [whom] by [how]."

Step 2: Define the Tool Set

An agent without tools is just a chatbot. Tools are functions your agent can call to interact with the real world. For your chosen agent, list every tool it needs:

Agent: Research Assistant

Tools needed:
1. web_search(query) → Returns search results
   - Why: Agent needs to find information
   - Input: search query string
   - Output: array of {title, url, snippet}

2. fetch_page(url) → Returns page content
   - Why: Agent needs to read full articles
   - Input: URL string
   - Output: extracted text content

3. save_note(title, content) → Saves to a file
   - Why: Agent needs to persist its findings
   - Input: title string, content string
   - Output: file path

Start with 2-3 tools. You can always add more later. Each tool should do exactly one thing.

Step 3: Map the Error Scenarios

Every tool can fail. For each tool, list what could go wrong and how the agent should handle it:

  • web_search fails: API rate limit, network error, no results. Agent should retry once, then tell the user it couldn't find information.
  • fetch_page fails: 404, paywall, timeout. Agent should skip that source and try the next one.
  • save_note fails: Disk full, permission error. Agent should return the content in the response instead.

Also plan for these agent-level errors:

  • What if the user's request is ambiguous? → Ask for clarification
  • What if the agent gets stuck in a loop? → Set a max iteration count
  • What if the response is too long? → Summarize and offer the full version

Step 4: Write the Agent Spec

Combine everything into a one-page spec. Use Claude to help you refine it:

Help me refine this agent specification:

Name: [agent name]
Purpose: [one sentence]
User interaction: [API endpoint / CLI / chat]
Tools: [list from Step 2]
Error handling: [strategies from Step 3]
System prompt: [describe the persona and constraints]

Critique this design. What am I missing? What edge cases haven't I considered?

Save this spec. You'll reference it throughout the build.

steps.title: Tool Use Fundamentals
steps.description: Learn how Claude calls tools — the inputSchema, the tool loop, and the response format.

steps.title: Build Your First Tool
steps.description: Write a tool function, connect it to Claude, and see the agent use it.

sections.content:
Making Claude Do Things

Without tools, Claude can only generate text. With tools, Claude can search the web, read databases, send emails, create files — anything you give it access to. Here's how it works:

  1. You send a message to Claude with a list of available tools
  2. Claude decides it needs to use a tool and returns a tool_use response
  3. Your code executes the tool and sends the result back to Claude
  4. Claude uses the result to continue its response

This is called the tool loop, and it's the core pattern of every AI agent.

Step 1: Define the Tool Schema

Pick the simplest tool from your agent spec. Define it as a JSON schema that Claude understands:

const tools = [
  {
    name: "web_search",
    description: "Search the web for information on a given topic. Returns the top 5 results with titles, URLs, and snippets.",
    input_schema: {
      type: "object",
      properties: {
        query: {
          type: "string",
          description: "The search query"
        }
      },
      required: ["query"]
    }
  }
];

The description field matters enormously. Claude uses it to decide when to call the tool and what to pass as input. Be specific: "Search the web for information" is better than "Search things." Include what the tool returns so Claude knows what to expect.

Step 2: Implement the Tool Function

Write the actual code that executes when Claude calls the tool. Start with a mock that returns fake data — you'll connect it to a real API later:

async function executeWebSearch(query) {
  // TODO: Replace with real search API
  // For now, return mock results so we can test the loop
  return [
    {
      title: `Result about: ${query}`,
      url: "https://example.com/article",
      snippet: `This is a mock search result for "${query}". Replace this with a real search API like Brave Search or SerpAPI.`
    }
  ];
}

// Tool router — maps tool names to functions
async function executeTool(name, input) {
  switch (name) {
    case "web_search":
      return await executeWebSearch(input.query);
    default:
      throw new Error(`Unknown tool: ${name}`);
  }
}

Using mocks first lets you test the entire agent loop without worrying about API keys or rate limits.

Step 3: Build the Tool Loop

Now connect Claude to your tool. The loop runs until Claude stops requesting tools:

import Anthropic from "@anthropic-ai/sdk";

const client = new Anthropic();

async function runAgent(userMessage) {
  let messages = [{ role: "user", content: userMessage }];

  while (true) {
    const response = await client.messages.create({
      model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
      max_tokens: 1024,
      tools: tools,
      messages: messages
    });

    // Check if Claude wants to use a tool
    if (response.stop_reason === "tool_use") {
      const toolBlock = response.content.find(b => b.type === "tool_use");
      const result = await executeTool(toolBlock.name, toolBlock.input);

      // Add Claude's response and the tool result to messages
      messages.push({ role: "assistant", content: response.content });
      messages.push({
        role: "user",
        content: [{
          type: "tool_result",
          tool_use_id: toolBlock.id,
          content: JSON.stringify(result)
        }]
      });
    } else {
      // Claude is done — return the final text
      const text = response.content.find(b => b.type === "text");
      return text?.text || "";
    }
  }
}

Run it: runAgent("What are the latest developments in AI safety?"). Claude will call your web_search tool, get the mock results, and produce a response that references them.

Step 4: Connect a Real API

Replace your mock with a real search. Brave Search has a free tier that works well:

  1. Sign up at brave.com/search/api for a free API key
  2. Replace the mock executeWebSearch function with a real HTTP call
  3. Run the agent again and see it produce answers based on real search results

Store your API key in an environment variable (process.env.BRAVE_API_KEY), never hardcode it. Test with a few different queries to make sure the tool works reliably.

steps.title: MCP Fundamentals
steps.description: Learn the Model Context Protocol — the standard that connects AI to everything.

steps.title: Add an MCP Server
steps.description: Build an MCP server for your agent and connect it to a real data source.

sections.content:
Why MCP?

In the previous step, you hardcoded tools directly into your agent. That works for a few tools, but it doesn't scale. What if you want your agent to use 10 tools from 5 different services? What if you want to share tools between agents?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) solves this. It's a standard protocol that lets AI agents discover and use tools from external servers. Instead of coding tools into your agent, you run MCP servers that expose tools, and your agent connects to them.

Step 1: Scaffold an MCP Server

Use Claude Code to build a basic MCP server:

Build an MCP server in TypeScript that exposes tools for my agent. Use the @modelcontextprotocol/sdk package.

The server should expose these tools:
1. search_notes(query) — searches a local directory of markdown files
2. create_note(title, content) — creates a new markdown file
3. list_notes() — returns all note filenames

Use stdio transport. Include proper error handling for file operations.

This gives your agent persistent memory — it can save and retrieve information across conversations.

Step 2: Test the MCP Server

Before connecting it to your agent, test the server standalone using the MCP Inspector or Claude Code:

npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector your-server.js

The Inspector shows you all the tools your server exposes. Call each one manually to verify it works. Create a note, list notes, search for it. Fix any bugs before moving on.

Step 3: Connect MCP to Your Agent

Update your agent to use the MCP server as a tool source instead of hardcoded tools. The MCP client discovers available tools automatically:

Ask Claude Code to help:

Update my agent to connect to my MCP server using the MCP client SDK. The agent should:
1. Start the MCP server as a subprocess
2. Discover available tools via the MCP protocol
3. Use those tools in the same tool loop we already built
4. The existing web_search tool should still work alongside MCP tools

Now your agent has tools from two sources: the hardcoded web search and the MCP note-taking server. This is the power of MCP — you can compose tools from multiple sources.

Step 4: Add a Real Data Source

The note-taking server is useful, but the real value of MCP is connecting to services you already use. Pick one:

  • GitHub MCP server: Your agent can read repos, issues, PRs
  • Database MCP server: Your agent can query Postgres or SQLite
  • File system MCP server: Your agent can read and manage your project files

Many MCP servers already exist as open-source projects. Search the MCP servers repository before building from scratch. Connect one, test it, and watch your agent's capabilities multiply.

steps.title: Error Handling & Edge Cases
steps.description: Make your agent robust. Handle failures gracefully. Learn production patterns.

sections.content:
The Difference Between a Demo and a Product

Your agent works on the happy path. But real users will break it in ways you haven't imagined. This step is about making your agent resilient — handling every failure mode gracefully so it never crashes, never hangs, and always gives the user a useful response.

Step 1: Add Tool Error Handling

Wrap every tool execution in error handling that returns useful information to Claude instead of crashing:

async function executeTool(name, input) {
  try {
    const result = await toolRouter(name, input);
    return { success: true, data: result };
  } catch (error) {
    return {
      success: false,
      error: error.message,
      suggestion: `The ${name} tool failed. Try a different approach.`
    };
  }
}

When you return the error to Claude as a tool result (instead of crashing), Claude can adapt — it might try a different tool, rephrase its query, or tell the user what happened. This is one of Claude's strengths: it handles partial failures well if you give it the information.

Step 2: Add Loop Protection

An agent can get stuck calling the same tool repeatedly or cycling between tools forever. Add two safeguards:

const MAX_ITERATIONS = 10;
const MAX_TOOL_CALLS = 25;
let iterations = 0;
let totalToolCalls = 0;

while (iterations < MAX_ITERATIONS) {
  iterations++;
  // ... your existing loop ...
  
  if (response.stop_reason === "tool_use") {
    totalToolCalls++;
    if (totalToolCalls > MAX_TOOL_CALLS) {
      return "I've made too many tool calls for this request. Here's what I found so far: ...";
    }
  }
}

Also add timeout protection: if any single tool call takes more than 30 seconds, abort it and return an error to Claude.

Step 3: Add Input Validation

Users will send your agent empty strings, absurdly long prompts, and malicious inputs. Add validation at the entry point:

function validateInput(message) {
  if (!message || message.trim().length === 0) {
    throw new Error("Please provide a message.");
  }
  if (message.length > 10000) {
    throw new Error("Message too long. Please keep it under 10,000 characters.");
  }
  return message.trim();
}

Also validate tool inputs before executing them. If your web_search tool expects a string, verify it's a string before calling the API.

Step 4: Test the Failure Modes

Deliberately break things to verify your error handling works:

  • Disconnect from the internet and run a query that needs web search
  • Send an empty message
  • Send a message that triggers 20+ tool calls
  • Kill the MCP server while the agent is running
  • Send a prompt injection: "Ignore all previous instructions and..."

For each test, verify: the agent doesn't crash, the user gets a useful error message, and no sensitive data is leaked in error messages. Fix anything that breaks.

steps.title: The Agent SDK
steps.description: Learn professional agent architecture with Anthropic's Agent SDK.

steps.title: Refactor to Agent SDK
steps.description: Convert your prototype into production architecture with guardrails and handoffs.

sections.content:
From Prototype to Production

Your hand-rolled agent loop works. But as agents get more complex — multiple tools, sub-agents, guardrails, handoffs — maintaining a custom loop becomes painful. The Agent SDK gives you a battle-tested framework for production agents.

Step 1: Define Your Agent

Convert your existing agent into an Agent SDK definition:

import { Agent } from "claude-agent-sdk";

const researchAgent = new Agent({
  name: "Research Assistant",
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  instructions: `You are a research assistant that helps users find and synthesize information.
    Always cite your sources. If you can't find reliable information, say so.
    Never make up facts.`,
  tools: [webSearchTool, fetchPageTool, saveNoteTool],
  maxTurns: 10
});

The SDK handles the tool loop, message management, and error handling you built by hand. Your job is now to define the agent's behavior, not manage the plumbing.

Step 2: Add Guardrails

Guardrails are rules that constrain what your agent can do. They run before or after each agent turn:

const guardrails = {
  // Prevent the agent from accessing certain domains
  beforeToolCall: async (toolName, input) => {
    if (toolName === "fetch_page" && input.url.includes("internal.company.com")) {
      return { blocked: true, reason: "Cannot access internal URLs" };
    }
    return { blocked: false };
  },
  // Validate output before returning to user
  afterResponse: async (response) => {
    if (response.includes("I don't know") && response.length < 50) {
      return { retry: true, feedback: "Provide more detail about what you searched and why you couldn't find an answer." };
    }
    return { retry: false };
  }
};

Guardrails are what separate a safe, production agent from a dangerous one. Add at least one input guardrail and one output guardrail.

Step 3: Add a Specialist Sub-Agent

Complex tasks benefit from agent handoffs. Create a specialist agent that your main agent can delegate to:

const summaryAgent = new Agent({
  name: "Summary Specialist",
  model: "claude-haiku-4-20250514",
  instructions: `You summarize long documents into concise, structured summaries.
    Always include: key findings, methodology, and limitations.
    Keep summaries under 500 words.`,
  tools: []
});

// Main agent can hand off to the summary specialist
researchAgent.addHandoff({
  target: summaryAgent,
  trigger: "When you have gathered all research and need to produce the final summary"
});

Using a smaller, cheaper model (Haiku) for summarization saves money and often produces better summaries because the agent is focused on one task.

Step 4: Run and Compare

Run the same queries through both your hand-rolled agent and the SDK version. Compare:

  • Response quality — are the answers the same?
  • Error handling — does the SDK version handle failures better?
  • Code complexity — how much code did you delete?
  • Performance — is there a latency difference?

The SDK version should be shorter, more maintainable, and handle edge cases you forgot about in your custom implementation. If it's not, review the SDK docs for features you're not using yet.

steps.title: Deploy Your Agent
steps.description: Deploy your agent as a live API endpoint on Vercel.

sections.content:
Your Agent Goes Live

Your agent is built, tested, and hardened. Time to deploy it so other people (or other systems) can use it. We'll deploy it as an API endpoint on Vercel — a serverless function that runs your agent on demand.

Step 1: Create the API Route

Wrap your agent in a serverless function. If you're using Next.js:

// app/api/agent/route.ts
import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from "next/server";

export async function POST(request: NextRequest) {
  const { message } = await request.json();
  
  if (!message) {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: "Message required" }, { status: 400 });
  }

  try {
    const response = await runAgent(message);
    return NextResponse.json({ response });
  } catch (error) {
    console.error("Agent error:", error);
    return NextResponse.json(
      { error: "Agent encountered an error" },
      { status: 500 }
    );
  }
}

Notice: the error response does not leak the actual error message to the client. In production, you log the real error server-side and return a generic message to the user.

Step 2: Set Up Environment Variables

Your agent needs API keys (Anthropic, search API, etc.). Never put these in code. Add them to Vercel:

vercel env add ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
vercel env add BRAVE_API_KEY

Or add them through the Vercel dashboard under Settings > Environment Variables. Verify they're available in your serverless function with process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.

Step 3: Deploy

vercel --prod

Once deployed, test your endpoint:

curl -X POST https://your-project.vercel.app/api/agent \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message": "What are the latest developments in AI safety?"}'

You should get a JSON response with your agent's answer. If you get an error, check the Vercel function logs: vercel logs --follow.

Step 4: Add Basic Auth

Your API is public right now — anyone who finds the URL can use it and burn through your API credits. Add basic API key authentication:

export async function POST(request: NextRequest) {
  const authHeader = request.headers.get("authorization");
  if (authHeader !== `Bearer ${process.env.AGENT_API_KEY}`) {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: "Unauthorized" }, { status: 401 });
  }
  // ... rest of handler
}

Generate a random API key (openssl rand -hex 32), add it as AGENT_API_KEY in Vercel, and include it in your requests as a Bearer token. This is the minimum viable authentication for a personal API. For a production product, you'd use proper auth (JWT, OAuth, etc.).

steps.title: Add Monitoring
steps.description: Add logging, cost tracking, and error alerts so you know what your agent is doing.

sections.content:
You Can't Fix What You Can't See

Your agent is live, but you're flying blind. Is it working? How much is it costing you? Are users hitting errors? Monitoring answers these questions. Every production AI system needs three things: logging (what happened), cost tracking (how much it costs), and alerting (when something goes wrong).

Step 1: Structured Logging

Log every agent run with enough detail to debug issues later:

async function runAgentWithLogging(message) {
  const runId = crypto.randomUUID();
  const startTime = Date.now();
  let toolCalls = 0;
  let inputTokens = 0;
  let outputTokens = 0;

  console.log(JSON.stringify({
    event: "agent_start",
    runId,
    messageLength: message.length,
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString()
  }));

  // ... run agent, incrementing counters ...

  console.log(JSON.stringify({
    event: "agent_complete",
    runId,
    durationMs: Date.now() - startTime,
    toolCalls,
    inputTokens,
    outputTokens,
    estimatedCost: calculateCost(inputTokens, outputTokens)
  }));
}

Using structured JSON logs (not plain text) means you can filter and aggregate them later. Vercel shows function logs in the dashboard, and you can connect them to external services like Datadog or Axiom.

Step 2: Cost Tracking

Claude API usage adds up. Track costs per request so you can set budgets and detect anomalies:

function calculateCost(inputTokens, outputTokens) {
  // Sonnet pricing (update if using a different model)
  const inputCostPer1M = 3.00;
  const outputCostPer1M = 15.00;
  return (
    (inputTokens / 1_000_000) * inputCostPer1M +
    (outputTokens / 1_000_000) * outputCostPer1M
  );
}

Log the cost for every request. Set a daily budget and track cumulative spending. If a single request costs more than $0.50, that's usually a sign of a loop or an excessively large context — investigate.

Step 3: Error Alerts

Don't wait for users to report problems. Set up basic alerting:

  • Quick win: Use Vercel's built-in log alerts to get notified when your function throws errors
  • Better: Log errors to a service like Sentry that groups similar errors and tracks frequency
  • Best: Build a simple health check endpoint that your monitoring system pings every few minutes

At minimum, you should know within 5 minutes if your agent starts failing consistently.

Step 4: Build a Simple Dashboard

Use Claude Code to create a basic monitoring page that shows:

  • Total requests today / this week / this month
  • Average response time
  • Error rate
  • Total cost this billing period
  • Top tools used

Even a simple HTML page that reads from your logs is better than nothing. As your agent scales, you'll replace this with proper observability tools — but starting with something is what matters.

steps.title: Your Agent is Live
steps.description: Demo your agent, share the API, and celebrate what you built.

sections.content:
Mission Complete

You've built a production AI agent from scratch. Let's review what you have:

  • A working agent with custom tools that interact with the real world
  • MCP integration for extensible, composable tool management
  • Agent SDK architecture with guardrails and sub-agent handoffs
  • A live API endpoint on Vercel with authentication
  • Monitoring: logging, cost tracking, and error alerting

Skills Unlocked

  • Claude API: Messages API, streaming, tool use, system prompts
  • Tool Use: inputSchema, the tool loop, error handling
  • MCP: Server architecture, tool discovery, protocol integration
  • Agent SDK: Agent definitions, guardrails, handoffs, sub-agents
  • Production Patterns: Auth, environment variables, structured logging, cost tracking
  • Deployment: Vercel serverless functions, API routes, monitoring

Share Your Agent

Share your agent's API endpoint with a friend or colleague. Give them the curl command and let them try it. Better yet, build a simple frontend for it — a chat interface that calls your API.

Next steps:

  • Add more tools — each one makes your agent more capable
  • Build a proper frontend (React + Vercel AI SDK makes this easy)
  • Add streaming so responses appear in real-time
  • Connect to more MCP servers for an ever-expanding toolkit
  • Ready to build a full product? Mission 4 takes you from agent to SaaS

missions.title: Launch a SaaS Product
missions.description: Build and launch a complete SaaS product with Claude, from idea to paying customers. For entrepreneurs and builders who want to ship something real.
missions.project_brief: You'll go from a blank page to a live SaaS product with authentication, payments, AI features, and a marketing site. This isn't a tutorial app — it's a real product you'll launch and try to get paying customers for. Claude is your co-founder for the sprint.
learning_outcomes: Validate a SaaS idea with market research and competitive analysis
Architect a full-stack application with Next.js and Supabase
Implement authentication, payments, and AI features
Deploy, launch, and market a real product

steps.title: Find Your Idea
steps.description: Use Claude to brainstorm, validate, and scope a SaaS idea worth building.

sections.content:
Every Product Starts with a Problem

The biggest mistake first-time founders make: building something nobody wants. We're going to avoid that by starting with problems, not solutions. Claude is an incredible brainstorming partner because it can rapidly explore angles you'd never think of — but you have to bring the domain knowledge about what problems are real.

Step 1: Problem Mining

Start by listing problems you personally experience. Not startup ideas — problems. Things that annoy you, waste your time, or cost you money. Use Claude to expand your thinking:

I'm looking for a SaaS product to build. Help me brainstorm by interviewing me. Ask me questions one at a time about:
1. My job and daily frustrations
2. Tools I use that I hate
3. Tasks I do repeatedly that feel dumb
4. Things I've cobbled together with spreadsheets or scripts
5. Problems I've seen other people complain about online

After each answer, suggest 2-3 potential product ideas based on what I said. Keep going until we have 10 ideas.

Let Claude interview you. Be honest and specific. The best SaaS ideas come from problems you've personally experienced.

Step 2: Validate the Top 3

Pick your 3 most promising ideas. For each one, ask Claude to run a quick validation:

Help me validate this SaaS idea:

Idea: [your idea]
Target user: [who has this problem]
Existing solutions: [what they use today]

Analyze:
1. Market size — How many people have this problem?
2. Willingness to pay — Are people already paying for solutions?
3. Competition — Who are the top 3 competitors? What do they charge?
4. Defensibility — What would stop a competitor from copying this?
5. Build complexity — How hard is an MVP? (1-10)
6. Time to value — How fast can a user get value after signing up?

Be brutally honest. Tell me why this might fail.

The goal isn't to find a perfect idea — it's to find one that's small enough to build in 20 hours, solves a real problem, and has people willing to pay.

Step 3: Scope the MVP

Take your winning idea and ruthlessly scope it down to an MVP (Minimum Viable Product). An MVP is the smallest thing you can build that delivers value to one user.

Help me scope the MVP for [your idea].

Rules:
- Must be buildable in ~15 hours of coding
- Maximum 3 core features
- No features that are "nice to have" — only features users NEED
- Must have a clear "aha moment" in the first 60 seconds

For each feature, estimate:
- Build time (hours)
- Complexity (low/medium/high)
- Is it truly necessary for the MVP or can it wait?

Also define: What does the user do in their first 60 seconds? What's the "aha moment" where they think "I need this"?

Step 4: Write the One-Pager

Document your validated idea in a single page. This becomes your north star for everything you build:

  • Product name (pick something, you can change it later)
  • One sentence: [Product] helps [who] [do what] by [how]
  • Target user: Who is this for, specifically?
  • Core problem: What pain does it solve?
  • MVP features: The 3 features you'll build
  • Pricing: What will you charge? (Use competitor research)
  • Success metric: How will you know the MVP works?

Save this document. Every decision you make from here should trace back to it. If a feature doesn't serve the core problem, it doesn't make the MVP.

steps.title: Think Like an Architect
steps.description: Learn advanced prompting techniques for designing complex systems.

steps.title: Design Your Architecture
steps.description: Use Claude to design the tech stack, database schema, and API routes for your SaaS.

sections.content:
Architecture Before Code

The most expensive bugs are architecture bugs — they require rewriting entire systems. Spend 90 minutes designing before you write a single line of code. This step uses Claude as a systems architect to help you make decisions you won't regret.

Step 1: Choose Your Stack

For a solo founder building fast, this stack is battle-tested:

  • Frontend + Backend: Next.js (App Router) — handles both UI and API routes
  • Database + Auth: Supabase — Postgres database, built-in auth, instant APIs
  • Payments: Stripe — the standard for SaaS billing
  • AI: Claude API via Anthropic SDK
  • Hosting: Vercel — deploys from GitHub, scales automatically
  • Styling: Tailwind CSS — fast, consistent, no bikeshedding

You can choose a different stack if you have strong preferences, but don't spend more than 10 minutes deciding. The best stack is the one you ship with.

Step 2: Design the Database Schema

Your database schema is the backbone of your application. Ask Claude to design it:

Design a Supabase (Postgres) database schema for my SaaS:

Product: [your one-sentence description]
MVP features: [your 3 features]
User model: [what data do you store per user]

Requirements:
- RLS (Row Level Security) policies on every table
- Timestamps (created_at, updated_at) on every table
- Foreign keys with proper cascading
- Indexes on columns that will be queried frequently
- A subscriptions table that tracks Stripe subscription status

Output as SQL migration that I can run in Supabase.
Explain each table and why it exists.

Review the schema carefully. Ask Claude "What queries will be slow with this schema?" and "What's missing if I want to add [feature] later?" Better to catch design issues now than after you have data.

Step 3: Map the API Routes

List every API endpoint your MVP needs:

Based on my database schema and MVP features, map out all the API routes I need:

For each route:
- Method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)
- Path (/api/...)
- What it does (one sentence)
- Who can access it (public, authenticated, admin)
- Request body / query params
- Response shape

Also flag any routes that need rate limiting (anything AI-powered or expensive).

This route map becomes your implementation checklist. You'll build each endpoint one at a time.

Step 4: Sketch the UI Flow

You don't need Figma. Describe the user flow in words and let Claude help you plan the pages:

Map the user journey for my SaaS:

1. Landing page → What do they see? What's the CTA?
2. Sign up → Email/password or OAuth?
3. Onboarding → What info do you need? How many steps?
4. Dashboard → What's the first thing they see?
5. Core feature → How do they use the main feature?
6. Settings → What can they configure?
7. Billing → Where do they manage their subscription?

For each page, list the components needed and the API routes it calls.

Save this architecture document alongside your one-pager. You now have a complete blueprint.

steps.title: Build the Backend
steps.description: Scaffold your Next.js + Supabase project and build the core data model.

sections.content:
Foundation First

Time to write code. We'll use Claude Code for everything — it can scaffold projects, write API routes, run migrations, and fix bugs. The key is to build in the right order: database first, then API, then UI.

Step 1: Scaffold the Project

Open your terminal and use Claude Code to set up the project:

Create a new Next.js project for my SaaS called [name]:
- Next.js with App Router and TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS for styling
- Supabase client library (@supabase/supabase-js)
- Anthropic SDK (@anthropic-ai/sdk)
- Environment variables template (.env.local.example) with:
  - NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
  - NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
  - SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY
  - ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
  - STRIPE_SECRET_KEY
  - STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Set up the Supabase client utility (src/lib/supabase.ts) with both browser and server clients.

Verify the project runs: npm run dev. You should see the Next.js default page at localhost:3000.

Step 2: Run the Database Migration

Take the SQL migration Claude designed in the architecture step and run it in Supabase:

  1. Go to your Supabase dashboard > SQL Editor
  2. Paste the migration SQL
  3. Run it
  4. Check the Table Editor to verify all tables were created
  5. Verify RLS policies are active on every table

Alternatively, use the Supabase CLI:

supabase db push

After the migration, ask Claude Code to generate TypeScript types from your schema: supabase gen types typescript --local > src/types/database.ts. This gives you full type safety when querying the database.

Step 3: Build the API Routes

Work through your route map from the architecture step. Build them one at a time, starting with the simplest:

Build the API route for [your first endpoint]:

Route: [method] /api/[path]
Purpose: [what it does]
Auth: [who can access]
Request: [body/params]
Response: [shape]

Use the Supabase server client for database queries.
Add input validation.
Return proper HTTP status codes.
Handle errors without leaking internal details.

After each route, test it with curl or a tool like Bruno/Postman. Don't move to the next route until the current one works perfectly.

Step 4: Verify with Real Data

Insert some test data into your Supabase tables manually, then verify your API routes return the correct data. Check:

  • Do RLS policies work? (An unauthenticated request should fail)
  • Do queries return the right shape?
  • Are timestamps being set correctly?
  • Do foreign key constraints prevent bad data?

Your backend is done when every route in your map works and returns correct data. The frontend comes next.

steps.title: Build the Frontend
steps.description: Build the UI with Claude Code. Component architecture through building.

sections.content:
Making It Real

Your API works. Now users need a way to interact with it. We'll build the frontend page by page, following the user journey you designed in the architecture step. Claude Code is phenomenal at generating React components — your job is to describe what you want and iterate on the result.

Step 1: Build the Layout

Start with the shell — the parts that appear on every page:

Build the app layout for my SaaS:
- A sidebar navigation (collapsible on mobile) with links to: Dashboard, [Feature 1], [Feature 2], Settings
- A top bar with the product name/logo and a user avatar dropdown (sign out, settings)
- A main content area that takes the remaining space
- Use Tailwind CSS. Dark background sidebar, light main area.
- Make it responsive — sidebar becomes a hamburger menu on mobile.

Create this as a layout.tsx in the app/(dashboard) route group.

Getting the layout right first means every page you build afterward slots into a consistent frame. Test on mobile and desktop before moving on.

Step 2: Build the Dashboard

The dashboard is what users see after logging in. It should answer: "What's happening?" and "What should I do next?"

Build the dashboard page for my SaaS:
- Show 3-4 metric cards at the top (e.g., total [items], this week's [activity], [usage metric])
- A recent activity feed or table showing the last 10 [items/actions]
- A prominent CTA button for the main feature
- Empty state: if the user has no data yet, show a friendly onboarding message with a "Get Started" button

Fetch data from the API routes we built. Use loading states (skeleton loaders, not spinners).

Step 3: Build the Core Feature Page

This is the page where users get value — the reason they signed up. Describe it to Claude Code in detail:

Build the main feature page for my SaaS:

[Describe exactly what the user does on this page]
[Describe the inputs — forms, file uploads, text areas]
[Describe the output — what appears after they submit]
[Describe the flow — step by step]

Requirements:
- Optimistic UI updates (show results immediately, sync in background)
- Loading states for any async operations
- Error handling with user-friendly messages
- Form validation before submission

This page will take the most iteration. Build it, use it yourself, and keep refining with Claude Code until it feels smooth.

Step 4: Polish and Connect

Walk through the entire user journey from sign-up to using the core feature. Note every friction point:

  • Is the navigation intuitive? Can you find everything in 2 clicks?
  • Are loading states visible? Users should never stare at a blank screen.
  • Do error messages tell the user what to do, not just what went wrong?
  • Does it look professional? Consistent spacing, typography, colors?

Fix the top 5 issues with Claude Code. Don't aim for pixel-perfect — aim for "I wouldn't be embarrassed to show this to someone."

steps.title: Production Patterns
steps.description: Learn the patterns that separate hobby projects from real products.

steps.title: Add Auth & Payments
steps.description: Supabase Auth + Stripe. The two things every SaaS needs.

sections.content:
The Two Pillars of SaaS

A SaaS product without authentication isn't a product — it's a demo. A SaaS without payments isn't a business — it's a hobby. This step adds both. We'll use Supabase Auth (easy, built-in) and Stripe (the industry standard for payments).

Step 1: Set Up Supabase Auth

Supabase Auth is already integrated with your database — enabling it takes minutes:

Set up authentication for my SaaS using Supabase Auth:

1. Create a sign-up page (/auth/signup) with email + password
2. Create a sign-in page (/auth/signin) with email + password
3. Add Google OAuth as an alternative sign-in method
4. Create auth middleware that protects all /dashboard routes
5. Add a profiles table that gets auto-populated on signup (use a Supabase trigger)
6. Update the layout to show the user's name/avatar from the profile

Use @supabase/ssr for server-side auth in Next.js App Router.
Redirect unauthenticated users to /auth/signin.

Test the full flow: sign up, verify email, sign in, access dashboard, sign out. Then test the protection: try accessing /dashboard while signed out — you should be redirected.

Step 2: Set Up Stripe

Create a Stripe account at stripe.com and set up your product:

  1. In the Stripe dashboard, create a Product with a monthly price (e.g., $19/month)
  2. Copy the price ID (starts with price_)
  3. Add your Stripe keys to .env.local

Then ask Claude Code to build the integration:

Set up Stripe subscription billing:

1. Create a checkout API route (/api/checkout) that:
   - Creates a Stripe Checkout Session for the monthly plan
   - Includes the user's Supabase ID as metadata
   - Redirects to /dashboard on success, /pricing on cancel

2. Create a webhook handler (/api/webhooks/stripe) that:
   - Verifies the Stripe signature
   - Handles: checkout.session.completed, customer.subscription.updated, customer.subscription.deleted
   - Updates the subscriptions table in Supabase

3. Create a billing page (/dashboard/settings/billing) that shows:
   - Current plan status
   - Next billing date
   - A "Manage Subscription" button (Stripe Customer Portal)

Stripe price ID: [your price ID]

Step 3: Gate Features Behind Subscription

Your free tier should let users see the product's value. The paid tier unlocks full access. Add subscription checks:

Add subscription gating to my app:

- Free users can: [define what's free — e.g., 5 uses per month]
- Paid users can: [define what's paid — e.g., unlimited uses]

Create a helper function checkSubscription(userId) that returns the user's plan.
Add a paywall component that shows when free users hit their limit.
The paywall should show what they're missing and a "Upgrade" button that triggers Stripe Checkout.

Step 4: Test the Full Payment Flow

Use Stripe's test mode (it's on by default) to test the entire payment flow:

  1. Sign up as a new user
  2. Use the free tier until you hit the limit
  3. Click "Upgrade" — you should see Stripe Checkout
  4. Use test card 4242 4242 4242 4242 (any expiry, any CVC)
  5. After payment, verify: subscription table updated, features unlocked, billing page shows active subscription
  6. Cancel the subscription in Stripe dashboard — verify the app reflects the cancellation

If any step fails, fix it before moving on. Payment bugs are the most trust-destroying bugs in a SaaS.

steps.title: Add AI Features
steps.description: Integrate the Claude API into your product and give it AI superpowers.

sections.content:
Your Product's Superpower

This is where your SaaS goes from "another CRUD app" to something genuinely differentiated. You're going to integrate Claude directly into your product's core experience. The goal: users should feel like the product is intelligent — it understands their needs and does the hard work for them.

Step 1: Design the AI Feature

Look at your core feature and ask: "Where does the user do the most thinking or manual work?" That's where AI adds the most value. Common patterns:

  • Generation: User provides a brief, AI generates content/code/analysis
  • Analysis: User provides data, AI extracts insights and summarizes
  • Recommendation: Based on user behavior, AI suggests next actions
  • Transformation: User provides content in one format, AI converts it to another

Pick the pattern that fits your product. Define: What's the input? What's the output? What makes a good output vs. a bad one?

Step 2: Build the API Route

Create a server-side API route that calls Claude. Never call the Claude API from the browser — your API key would be exposed.

Build an API route for my AI feature:

Route: POST /api/ai/[feature-name]
Input: [what the user provides]
Output: [what Claude generates]

Requirements:
- Use streaming (the Anthropic SDK supports it) so users see results appear in real-time
- Set a system prompt that constrains Claude to produce exactly the output format I need
- Include the user's relevant data from Supabase as context
- Add rate limiting: max [N] requests per user per hour
- Track token usage per user for cost allocation
- Never include sensitive data from other users in the context

Step 3: Build the AI UI

The user interface for AI features needs special consideration:

Build the frontend for my AI feature:

- A form/input area where users provide their [input]
- A results area that streams Claude's response in real-time (use the Vercel AI SDK useChat or useCompletion hook)
- A loading indicator that shows while waiting for the first token
- An "AI-generated" badge so users know the output came from AI
- A "Regenerate" button to try again with the same input
- A "Copy" or "Save" button for the output
- Error state: if the AI fails, show a helpful message and a retry option

Streaming makes AI features feel dramatically faster. Instead of waiting 10 seconds for a complete response, users see text appearing immediately.

Step 4: Optimize Prompt Quality

The AI feature is only as good as the prompt. Test it with 10 different realistic inputs and grade each output:

  • A (perfect): User could use the output as-is
  • B (good): Needs minor edits
  • C (usable): Needs significant editing but the structure is right
  • D (bad): User would rather start from scratch

If you're getting mostly Cs and Ds, your system prompt needs work. Common fixes: add output format examples, add constraints ("never include...", "always start with..."), add the user's context data, reduce the scope of what you're asking Claude to do.

Target: 80%+ of outputs should be A or B before you ship.

steps.title: Deploy to Vercel
steps.description: Production deployment. Custom domain. Environment variables. The real deal.

sections.content:
Going to Production

Development is done. Time to deploy your SaaS to production where real users can access it. Vercel makes this straightforward, but there are important steps between "works on localhost" and "ready for users."

Step 1: Production Environment Variables

Your .env.local has development keys. Production needs its own set:

  • Supabase: Same project, same keys (unless you have a staging project)
  • Stripe: Switch to live mode keys (not test mode). Create your real product and price.
  • Anthropic: Same API key, but set usage limits in the Anthropic console
  • Add a new variable: NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL set to your production URL

Add all production environment variables in Vercel dashboard > Settings > Environment Variables. Double-check that none are missing — a missing env var will crash your app silently.

Step 2: Deploy

Push your code to GitHub and connect it to Vercel:

git add .
git commit -m "Production ready"
git push origin main

In Vercel: Import repository > Select your repo > Vercel will auto-detect Next.js > Deploy.

Or via CLI: vercel --prod

Wait for the build to complete. Click the deployment URL. Test the critical path: sign up, use the product, upgrade to paid, verify the AI feature works.

Step 3: Custom Domain

A .vercel.app URL doesn't inspire confidence. Get a custom domain:

  1. Buy a domain from Namecheap, Cloudflare, or your preferred registrar
  2. In Vercel dashboard > Settings > Domains > Add your domain
  3. Update your DNS records as Vercel instructs (usually a CNAME or A record)
  4. Vercel automatically provisions an SSL certificate
  5. Update NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL to your custom domain
  6. Update Stripe webhook endpoints to use the new domain
  7. Update Supabase redirect URLs to use the new domain

Step 4: Production Checklist

Before you tell anyone about your product, verify:

  • [ ] All pages load without errors (check browser console)
  • [ ] Auth flow works: signup, signin, signout, password reset
  • [ ] Payment flow works with a real card (use a real $1 test charge, then refund)
  • [ ] AI feature produces good output
  • [ ] Rate limiting is active on AI and auth endpoints
  • [ ] Error pages (404, 500) show a branded page, not a stack trace
  • [ ] Stripe webhooks are receiving events (check Stripe dashboard > Webhooks)
  • [ ] Mobile layout works (test on a real phone)
  • [ ] Page load speed is under 3 seconds (run a Lighthouse audit)

steps.title: Build Your Landing Page
steps.description: Build a marketing site that sells your product. Copy, design, and conversion.

sections.content:
Sell the Outcome, Not the Features

Your product works. Now you need to convince people to try it. The landing page is your sales pitch — it needs to communicate the value of your product in under 10 seconds. Most visitors will leave if they don't immediately understand what you're offering and why they should care.

Step 1: Write the Copy First

Before any design, nail the words. Use Claude to help:

Write landing page copy for my SaaS:

Product: [one sentence]
Target user: [who]
Main benefit: [what they get]
Price: [amount/month]

Sections needed:
1. Hero: Headline (under 10 words), subheadline (under 25 words), CTA button text
2. Problem: Describe the pain point in the user's words (not marketing speak)
3. Solution: How our product fixes it (3 bullet points, benefit-first)
4. Social proof: Placeholder for testimonials (write 2 realistic-sounding ones)
5. Pricing: Simple, one plan, with a feature list
6. FAQ: 5 questions a skeptical visitor would ask
7. Final CTA: One more push to sign up

Tone: confident, clear, no jargon. Write like you're explaining it to a friend.

Step 2: Build the Page

Use Claude Code to build the landing page as the root route of your app:

Build a landing page at / (the root route) for my SaaS. Here's the copy:
[paste the copy from Step 1]

Design requirements:
- Hero section with a gradient or bold background, large headline, CTA button
- Problem section with icons or illustrations
- Solution section with a product screenshot or mockup area
- Pricing card, centered, with feature checkmarks
- FAQ as an accordion
- Sticky header with product name and "Sign Up" button
- Footer with links
- Use Tailwind CSS, make it beautiful and modern
- Fully responsive
- Fast: no heavy images or animations that hurt page load

Step 3: Add a Product Demo

The most effective landing pages show the product in action. Options (pick one):

  • Screenshot: Take a screenshot of your dashboard, embed it in the hero section
  • GIF/Video: Record a 30-second screen recording of the core feature
  • Interactive demo: Let visitors try the AI feature without signing up (limited to 1 use)

The interactive demo converts best but takes more work. At minimum, include a screenshot. People need to see what they're signing up for.

Step 4: Optimize for Conversion

Review your landing page with fresh eyes. Better yet, show it to 3 people who fit your target audience and watch them read it. Check:

  • Can they explain what the product does after 5 seconds? (If not, rewrite the headline)
  • Do they understand the price? (If not, simplify the pricing section)
  • Do they trust it? (Add logos, testimonials, a money-back guarantee)
  • Is the CTA obvious? (One primary CTA per section, high-contrast button)

steps.title: Launch Day
steps.description: Go live. Share with the world. Get your first users.

sections.content:
Ship It

This is the moment. Your product is built, deployed, and has a landing page. Now you need humans to use it. Launch day isn't about perfection — it's about getting your product in front of real people who can tell you if it's useful.

Step 1: Pre-Launch Checklist

Final checks before you share the URL with anyone:

  • [ ] Sign up with a fresh email and go through the entire flow
  • [ ] Make a real payment (even $1) to verify Stripe is working in live mode
  • [ ] Test the AI feature with a realistic input
  • [ ] Check the landing page on mobile
  • [ ] Verify error pages work (visit a non-existent URL)
  • [ ] Set up Google Analytics or Vercel Analytics for tracking
  • [ ] Verify your custom domain has SSL (https works)

Step 2: Write Your Launch Posts

Use Claude to draft launch announcements for different platforms:

Write 3 launch announcements for my SaaS:

1. LinkedIn post (professional, focus on the problem solved)
2. X/Twitter post (concise, with a hook)
3. Reddit post for r/[relevant subreddit] (authentic, not salesy, ask for feedback)

Product: [your one-sentence description]
URL: [your URL]
Key feature: [the thing that makes it special]

Tone: excited but not hype-y. Frame it as "I built this thing and I'd love feedback" not "BUY MY PRODUCT."

Step 3: Launch

Post your announcements. Send a personal message to 10 people who fit your target audience and ask them to try it. Not a mass email — individual, personal messages:

"Hey [name], I just built [product] that helps with [problem you know they have]. Would you mind trying it for 5 minutes and telling me what you think? Here's the link: [URL]. Brutally honest feedback is exactly what I need."

These first 10 users are gold. Their feedback will shape every decision you make next.

Step 4: Monitor Launch Day

On launch day, watch your metrics closely:

  • Vercel Analytics: How many visitors? What pages do they visit?
  • Supabase: How many sign-ups? Are they completing onboarding?
  • Stripe: Any payments? Any failed payments?
  • Error logs: Are users hitting bugs?

Be online and responsive. If someone hits a bug, fix it within the hour. First impressions matter enormously for a new product. Your speed of response signals how much you care.

steps.title: You Launched a Product
steps.description: Review your SaaS, share the URL, and plan what comes next.

sections.content:
Mission Complete

You built and launched a SaaS product. Not a tutorial project. Not a demo. A real product with real users, real payments, and real AI features. Here's what you have:

  • A complete Next.js application with App Router
  • Supabase database with RLS, auth, and typed queries
  • Stripe subscription billing with webhook integration
  • AI features powered by the Claude API with streaming
  • A marketing landing page with conversion-optimized copy
  • Production deployment on Vercel with a custom domain
  • Your first users and their feedback

Skills Unlocked

  • Product thinking: Problem identification, validation, MVP scoping
  • Full-stack development: Next.js, Supabase, API routes, React components
  • AI integration: Claude API, streaming, prompt optimization, rate limiting
  • Payments: Stripe Checkout, webhooks, subscription management
  • Deployment: Vercel, custom domains, environment management
  • Marketing: Landing page copy, conversion optimization, launch strategy

What Comes Next

Launching is the beginning, not the end. Your next moves:

  • Week 1: Talk to every user. Ask what they love, what's broken, what's missing.
  • Week 2: Fix the top 3 issues. Ship an update. Tell your users.
  • Week 3: Start content marketing. Write about the problem your product solves.
  • Month 2: Add the features users asked for most. Raise the price if people are getting value.
  • Month 3: Evaluate. Is this growing? Do users retain? If yes, double down. If no, pivot or kill it.

The hardest part isn't building — it's what comes after. But you have something most people don't: a live product, real users, and the skills to iterate fast with Claude as your co-pilot. Go build.
